func TestArchiveGetWithArchiveFallsBack(t *testing.T) {
	s, _ := archiveTestStore(t)
	old := time.Now().UTC().Add(-60 * 24 * time.Hour)
	require.NoError(t, s.WriteAll([]Bead{
		closedBeadAt("ddx-archived", "to be archived", old),
		{
			ID:        "ddx-active",
			Title:     "still active",
			Status:    StatusOpen,
			Priority:  2,
			IssueType: DefaultType,
			CreatedAt: old,
			UpdatedAt: old,
		},
	}))

	policy := defaultArchivePolicy()
	policy.MinActiveCount = 0
	moved, err := s.Archive(policy)
	require.NoError(t, err)
	require.Equal(t, []string{"ddx-archived"}, moved)

	// AC2: show resolves IDs from either collection.
	b, err := s.GetWithArchive(testCtx(), "ddx-archived")
	require.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, "to be archived", b.Title)

	b, err = s.GetWithArchive(testCtx(), "ddx-active")
	require.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, "still active", b.Title)

	_, err = s.GetWithArchive(testCtx(), "ddx-missing")
	assert.Error(t, err)
}

func TestStoreGetWithArchive_UsesSuppliedContext(t *testing.T) {
	t.Parallel()
	s, _ := archiveTestStore(t)
	require.NoError(t, s.WriteAll([]Bead{
		closedBeadAt("ddx-archived", "archived", time.Now().UTC().Add(-60*24*time.Hour)),
	}))

	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
	cancel()

	_, err := s.GetWithArchive(ctx, "ddx-archived")
	require.Error(t, err)
}

func TestArchiveReadyAndBlockedQueryActiveOnly(t *testing.T) {
	s, _ := archiveTestStore(t)
	old := time.Now().UTC().Add(-60 * 24 * time.Hour)
	require.NoError(t, s.WriteAll([]Bead{
		closedBeadAt("ddx-archived", "archived", old),
		{
			ID:        "ddx-open",
			Title:     "ready",
			Status:    StatusOpen,
			Priority:  2,
			IssueType: DefaultType,
			CreatedAt: old,
			UpdatedAt: old,
		},
	}))
	policy := defaultArchivePolicy()
	policy.MinActiveCount = 0
	_, err := s.Archive(policy)
	require.NoError(t, err)

	// AC4: ready/blocked only consider the active collection.
	ready, err := s.Ready()
	require.NoError(t, err)
	require.Len(t, ready, 1)
	assert.Equal(t, "ddx-open", ready[0].ID)

	blocked, err := s.Blocked()
	require.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Empty(t, blocked)
}

When `ddx work --watch` runs against a `ddx server` that is partially
unavailable (e.g. TLS handshake failure, agent-service connection
refusal, persistent route-resolution failures with no candidates), the
worker treats every individual bead as the failure unit. Each attempt
gets flagged with `operator_attention` via the progress-watchdog or
`execution_failed` via the routing preflight, then the next bead is
tried, then the next.

Result: a single server-side problem (one bad TLS cert, an expired
provider credential, a stale model catalog) silently turns the entire
ready queue into "needs human" beads, even though the *beads* are fine
— the *server* is broken. The worker keeps idling, the operator sees
"0 closures in 24h" with no clear signal that this is environmental.

Observed concretely on niflheim on 2026-05-31:

- `ddx server` was serving a TLS cert from
  `$DDX_PROJECT_ROOT/.ddx/server/tls/cert.pem` (Apr 17 fingerprint)
  while the client `ddx work` pinned the cert in `~/.ddx/server/tls/`
  (May 18 fingerprint). The server logged
  `http: TLS handshake error from 127.0.0.1:XXXXX: remote error: tls:
  bad certificate` every ~5 min for ~36 hours.
- Every `niflheim-71365a9f` attempt during that window failed with
  `ResolveRoute: no viable routing candidate: 16 candidates rejected,
  outcome_reason=no_viable_provider`.
- After 15+ attempts the worker flagged the bead for operator
  attention, then quietly idled with `execution-ready=0 blocked=47
  operator-attention=17 needs-human/investigation=10`. No clear
  signal to the operator that the *server* was the cause.

**Desired behavior**

The worker should detect server-level failures separately from
bead-level failures. Two-tier classification:

1. **Bead-level failure** (current behavior): the bead's tests/lint
   fail, the agent commits something invalid, etc. → mark the bead
   `execution_failed`, retry per policy, eventually
   `operator-attention`.
2. **Server-level failure** (new): TLS handshake error, agent-service
   connection refused, persistent `no_viable_provider` across ≥ K
   distinct beads in the last M minutes (default K=3, M=10) → mark
   the *server* unavailable, NOT the individual beads. Loop into a
   probe-and-resume mode: every 30 s ping
   `https://server/api/health`; the moment the server returns 200
   and a small `ddx run --text` smoke test succeeds, re-enter the
   queue. None of the beads attempted during the server-failure
   window get penalized.

The server-failure backoff prevents the per-bead operator-attention
flagging storm we observed. It also makes the actual root cause
visible — `ddx work` should emit a single distinctive log line
("server unreachable: holding queue until /api/health returns") and
expose that state via `ddx work status` so the operator notices it.

**In-scope files (likely; verify before edit)**

- `cli/internal/agent/service_run.go` — wraps `ResolveRoute` →
  classification site for routing failures.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead*.go` — the place where
  `no_viable_provider` is turned into `outcome_reason` for the bead.
- The watcher loop in `cli/internal/work/` (or wherever `ddx work
  --watch` lives) — needs the new server-unavailable state machine.
- `cli/internal/agent/` — health-probe helper.
- New unit tests covering: (a) K consecutive `no_viable_provider`
  across distinct bead IDs trips server-failure mode; (b)
  server-failure backoff doesn't penalize the beads it was about to
  try; (c) successful health probe transitions back to normal queue
  drain; (d) TLS handshake error from the server is classified as
  server-level regardless of bead.

**Acceptance**

1. `go test ./cli/internal/agent/... ./cli/internal/work/...` passes
   with the new tests above.
2. `lefthook run pre-commit` (or equivalent gates) green.
3. Manual smoke: stop `ddx server`, run `ddx work --watch`; observe
   the worker emits "server unreachable" within 30 s and does NOT
   flag any beads for operator attention while the server is down.
   Restart `ddx server`; observe queue drain resumes.

**Out of scope**

- Auto-repairing server-side issues (TLS cert refresh, credential
  rotation). Operator still has to fix the server. The worker just
  has to detect and not amplify the failure.
- Multi-server failover.
- Changing the existing per-bead `operator-attention` semantics —
  those stay for true bead-level failures.

**Severity**: P1. Prevents quiet queue-stall pattern that wastes
operator hours on triage when the actual cause is environmental.     </description>     <acceptance> See bead body — 3 verifiable AC items: unit tests covering the four classification scenarios; lefthook green; manual smoke confirming worker emits 'server unreachable' instead of flagging beads when server is down.     </acceptance>     <labels>area:work, area:agent, kind:reliability, kind:operator-experience</labels>     <metadata base-rev="9f0133e3de703678486bf5caf721001f87b45551" bundle=".ddx/executions/20260607T082129-7daa40f2"/>   </bead>   <governing>     <note>No governing references were pre-resolved. - Cross-reference each AC to concrete evidence (test, file, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Run git/index mutations sequentially; don't parallelize `git add`, `git commit`, or staging/commit commands.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- If `lefthook run pre-commit` depends on staged files, rerun it after staging the exact commit set. A `no-staged-files` run is not acceptance evidence.
- Commit exactly once when green; subject ends with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- Current-bead lifecycle is orchestrator-owned. Do not run `ddx bead update <bead-id> --claim`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --status <status>`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --unclaim`, or `ddx bead close <bead-id>`. Step 0 allows `ddx bead create`, `ddx bead dep add` for child-to-child or sibling/replacement edges, and `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
- If you cannot finish, write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path before exiting. No commit or rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`.

## no_changes contract

The `no_changes_rationale.txt` file under the bead metadata `bundle` path must contain one of:

- `verification_command: <cmd>` — repo cwd; exit 0 closes, nonzero rejects.
- `status: open` + `reason: <retryable>` — open, smart retry.
- `status: proposed` + `reason: <operator needed>` — operator lane.
- `status: blocked` + `reason: <external blocker>` — blocked lane.

No pseudo-statuses. Bare rationales and `status: needs_investigation` are rejected.

For orchestrator decomposition, add `orchestrator_action: decompose` alongside `status: open`.

## Reports

Reports go under the bead metadata `bundle` path in `.ddx/executions/`. **Never write reports to `/tmp` or outside the repo**. Use a named in-repo path when given; otherwise write `<short-name>.md` there. Commit it with the code.

## Mode and overrides

DDX_MODE=bead_execution: edit code/docs for bead AC. Only broad queue-steward default is overridden; tracker, merge-policy, verification, safety stay active. Bead description/AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and defaults.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other instruction files unless the bead asks.

## Long-running matrix/benchmark beads

For expensive commands (benchmarks, load tests, validation > 60s per variant):

- Write a matrix plan before launching expensive commands: list required configs, output paths, completion criteria.
- Do not re-run the same long-running command unless: (1) the command fingerprint changed (args/env/config), AND (2) you document why prior output is invalid and what changed.
- If a long-running command times out or is incomplete, exit with `status: open` + retryable `reason` in `no_changes_rationale.txt`. Do not silently retry; let the orchestrator decide.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator.   </instructions>   <bead id="ddx-658cb394">     <title>ddx work: detect server-level failures separately from bead-level (don't flag beads when server is unavailable)</title>     <description> **Problem**

When `ddx work --watch` runs against a `ddx server` that is partially
unavailable (e.g. TLS handshake failure, agent-service connection
refusal, persistent route-resolution failures with no candidates), the
worker treats every individual bead as the failure unit. Each attempt
gets flagged with `operator_attention` via the progress-watchdog or
`execution_failed` via the routing preflight, then the next bead is
tried, then the next.

Result: a single server-side problem (one bad TLS cert, an expired
provider credential, a stale model catalog) silently turns the entire
ready queue into "needs human" beads, even though the *beads* are fine
— the *server* is broken. The worker keeps idling, the operator sees
"0 closures in 24h" with no clear signal that this is environmental.

Observed concretely on niflheim on 2026-05-31:

- `ddx server` was serving a TLS cert from
  `$DDX_PROJECT_ROOT/.ddx/server/tls/cert.pem` (Apr 17 fingerprint)
  while the client `ddx work` pinned the cert in `~/.ddx/server/tls/`
  (May 18 fingerprint). The server logged
  `http: TLS handshake error from 127.0.0.1:XXXXX: remote error: tls:
  bad certificate` every ~5 min for ~36 hours.
- Every `niflheim-71365a9f` attempt during that window failed with
  `ResolveRoute: no viable routing candidate: 16 candidates rejected,
  outcome_reason=no_viable_provider`.
- After 15+ attempts the worker flagged the bead for operator
  attention, then quietly idled with `execution-ready=0 blocked=47
  operator-attention=17 needs-human/investigation=10`. No clear
  signal to the operator that the *server* was the cause.

**Desired behavior**

The worker should detect server-level failures separately from
bead-level failures. Two-tier classification:

1. **Bead-level failure** (current behavior): the bead's tests/lint
   fail, the agent commits something invalid, etc. → mark the bead
   `execution_failed`, retry per policy, eventually
   `operator-attention`.
2. **Server-level failure** (new): TLS handshake error, agent-service
   connection refused, persistent `no_viable_provider` across ≥ K
   distinct beads in the last M minutes (default K=3, M=10) → mark
   the *server* unavailable, NOT the individual beads. Loop into a
   probe-and-resume mode: every 30 s ping
   `https://server/api/health`; the moment the server returns 200
   and a small `ddx run --text` smoke test succeeds, re-enter the
   queue. None of the beads attempted during the server-failure
   window get penalized.

The server-failure backoff prevents the per-bead operator-attention
flagging storm we observed. It also makes the actual root cause
visible — `ddx work` should emit a single distinctive log line
("server unreachable: holding queue until /api/health returns") and
expose that state via `ddx work status` so the operator notices it.

**In-scope files (likely; verify before edit)**

- `cli/internal/agent/service_run.go` — wraps `ResolveRoute` →
  classification site for routing failures.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead*.go` — the place where
  `no_viable_provider` is turned into `outcome_reason` for the bead.
- The watcher loop in `cli/internal/work/` (or wherever `ddx work
  --watch` lives) — needs the new server-unavailable state machine.
- `cli/internal/agent/` — health-probe helper.
- New unit tests covering: (a) K consecutive `no_viable_provider`
  across distinct bead IDs trips server-failure mode; (b)
  server-failure backoff doesn't penalize the beads it was about to
  try; (c) successful health probe transitions back to normal queue
  drain; (d) TLS handshake error from the server is classified as
  server-level regardless of bead.

**Acceptance**

1. `go test ./cli/internal/agent/... ./cli/internal/work/...` passes
   with the new tests above.
2. `lefthook run pre-commit` (or equivalent gates) green.
3. Manual smoke: stop `ddx server`, run `ddx work --watch`; observe
   the worker emits "server unreachable" within 30 s and does NOT
   flag any beads for operator attention while the server is down.
   Restart `ddx server`; observe queue drain resumes.

**Out of scope**

- Auto-repairing server-side issues (TLS cert refresh, credential
  rotation). Operator still has to fix the server. The worker just
  has to detect and not amplify the failure.
- Multi-server failover.
- Changing the existing per-bead `operator-attention` semantics —
  those stay for true bead-level failures.

**Severity**: P1. Prevents quiet queue-stall pattern that wastes
operator hours on triage when the actual cause is environmental.     </description>     <acceptance> See bead body — 3 verifiable AC items: unit tests covering the four classification scenarios; lefthook green; manual smoke confirming worker emits 'server unreachable' instead of flagging beads when server is down.     </acceptance>     <labels>area:work, area:agent, kind:reliability, kind:operator-experience</labels>     <metadata base-rev="9f0133e3de703678486bf5caf721001f87b45551" bundle=".ddx/executions/20260607T082129-7daa40f2"/>   </bead>   <governing>     <note>No governing references were pre-resolved.
